summary:
The Xinhai Revolution of 1911–1912 overthrew the Qing dynasty and ended over two thousand years of imperial rule in China, establishing the [[event:3321|Republic of China]]. The revolution was triggered by a military uprising at Wuchang in Hubei province on October 10, 1911 — a date still celebrated as the National Day of the Republic of China (Taiwan). The uprising rapidly spread as provincial governments declared independence from the Qing central government, and the dynasty proved unable to mount an effective military response.

The revolution brought together diverse forces: military reformers trained in modern methods, intellectuals influenced by nationalist and republican ideas, secret societies, overseas Chinese communities, and provincial elites who had developed autonomous power bases during the late Qing period. Sun Yat-sen, leader of the Revolutionary Alliance and the dominant figure of Chinese republicanism, was in the United States when the revolution broke out but returned to be inaugurated as provisional president of the Republic of China in January 1912.

The Qing emperor Puyi abdicated in February 1912, ending the dynasty. However, Sun Yat-sen transferred the presidency to Yuan Shikai, a powerful military commander who had negotiated the abdication, in hopes of preventing civil war. Yuan's subsequent attempts to establish personal dictatorship and even restore the monarchy accelerated the fragmentation of political authority that led to the warlord period of Chinese politics. The Xinhai Revolution thus marks the end of imperial China without establishing stable republican governance, initiating a prolonged period of political turbulence.
